title: East Riding of Yorkshire Council
text:
Reference:  IC-393971-B4K5 
 
 1 
Freedom of Information Act 2000 (FOIA) 
Decision notice 
 
    
Date: 13 March 2026 
  
Public Authority: East Riding of Yorkshire Council 
Address: County Hall 
Cross Street 
Beverley 
East Yorkshire 
HU17 9BA 
  
 
 
Decision (including any steps ordered) 
1. The complainant has requested information about the total amount 
collected by Bridlington Park and Ride parking machines during 5-12 
April 2025. East Riding of Yorkshire Council (the council) refused to 
disclose the requested information citing section 31(1)(a) of FOIA (law 
enforcement) as its basis for doing so. 
2. The Commissioner’s decision is that the council has correctly applied 
section 31(1)(a) of FOIA to withhold the requested information and that 
the public interest favours maintaining the exemption. 
3. The Commissioner does not require further steps, as a result of this 
decision notice. 
 
 
 
 
 

Reference:  IC-393971-B4K5 
 
 2 
Request and response 
4. On 16 April 2025, the complainant wrote to the council and requested 
information in the following terms: 
“I request you accept an FOI: The amount taken by the parking 
machines in Bridlington Park & Ride for the period 5-12th April this 
year.” 
5. On 14 May 2025, the council provided its response, in which it confirmed 
that it held the requested information but that it was being withheld 
under section 31 of FOIA. 
6. Upon receiving this response, the complainant asked the council to 
conduct an internal review, on 14 May 2025. On 16 May 2025, the 
council provided its internal review in which it maintained its original 
position. 
Scope of the case 
7. The complainant contacted the Commissioner on 12 June 2025 to 
complain about the way their request for information had been handled. 
8. During his investigation, the Commissioner queried which part of section 
31 the council was relying on to withhold the requested information. The 
council confirmed that it was relying upon section 31(1)(a). 
9. Therefore, the Commissioner considers that the scope of his 
investigation is to consider the application of section 31(1)(a) of FOIA to 
the requested information. 
Reasons for decision 
Section 31 - law enforcement 
10. Section 31(1)(a) of FOIA allows a public authority to withhold 
information if its disclosure under the Act would, or would be likely to, 
prejudice the prevention or detection of crime. 
11. In order for section 31 to be engaged, the following criteria must be 
met: 
 

Reference:  IC-393971-B4K5 
 
 3 
• the actual harm which the public authority claims would, or would be 
likely to occur if the withheld information was disclosed has to relate 
to the applicable interests within the relevant exemptions (in this 
case, the prevention or detection of crime); 
• the public authority must be able to demonstrate that some causal 
relationship exists between the potential disclosure of the information 
being withheld and the prejudice which the exemption is designed to 
protect. Furthermore, the resultant prejudice, which is alleged must 
be real, actual or of substance; and, 
• it is necessary to establish whether the level of likelihood of prejudice 
being relied upon by the public authority is met – i.e. disclosure 
‘would be likely’ to result in prejudice or disclosure ‘would’ result in 
prejudice. 
12. In his published guidance, the Commissioner recognises that section 
31(1)(a) will cover all aspects of the prevention and detection of crime. 
13. Consideration of the exemption at section 31 is a two-stage process; 
even if the exemption is engaged, the information must be disclosed 
unless the public interest in maintaining the exemption outweighs the 
public interest in disclosure. 
The applicable interest 
14. The first step in considering whether this exemption is engaged is to 
consider whether the actual harm claimed   is relevant to ‘the prevention 
or detection of crime’ as mentioned in section 31(1)(a). 
15. In submissions to the Commissioner, the council explained that 
disclosure of the requested information could “provide operational 
patterns of high periods of cash collections in relation to parking fares or 
identify potential vulnerabilities in our payment methods that could be 
exploited.” This would, the council explained, make it more vulnerable to 
crime. 
16. The Commissioner is satisfied that the prejudice the council is 
envisaging in this case, is relevant to the particular interests which 
section 31(1)(a) is designed to protect. 
The nature of the prejudice 
17. The Commissioner next considered whether the council have 
demonstrated a causal link between the disclosure of the withheld 
information and the prejudice that section 31(1)(a) is designed to 
protect. In his view, disclosure must be at least capable of harming the 
interest in some way, ie, have a damaging or detrimental effect on it. 

Reference:  IC-393971-B4K5 
 
 4 
18. In submissions to the Commissioner, the council explained that the 
complainant had requested information relating to a “specific period of 
time…and a specific location”, and that the site uses a range of payment 
methods, including cash, card and permit schemes. 
19. The council further explained that if the information was to be disclosed 
it would “allow individuals to identify patterns or trends” within its 
income streams at specific car park locations. 
20. It added that such patterns may differ from what might normally be 
expected at certain times of the year, for example during bank holidays 
or school holidays. 
21. In evidencing this causal link, the council referred to “widely published 
news articles on the rise of parking scams linked to fake QR codes being 
placed on parking machines.” It argued that disclosing income figures 
for a limited timeframe would “open the council up to being targeted on 
similar dates/time periods.” 
Likelihood of prejudice 
22. The council in its initial response and internal review response used the 
word “could.” 
23. The Commissioner has therefore interpreted this to mean that the 
prejudice, as described above, ‘would be likely’ to occur if the withheld 
information were to be disclosed. The Commissioner accepts that this is 
a credible level of likelihood. 
Is the exemption engaged? 
24. The Commissioner notes that the council did not provide any news 
articles to evidence “the rise of parking scams linked to fake QR codes 
being placed on parking machines.” 
25. However, through his own brief online enquiries, the Commissioner 
identified a range of national and local articles which have reported on 
this issue. 
26. The Commissioner notes the complainant’s concerns that they have not 
asked for “how and when the machines are emptied or cashed up”. 
27. However, the Commissioner considers that the risk extends beyond the 
routine emptying of the machines. Disclosure of the withheld 
information could provide intelligence to individuals with criminal intent 
regarding peak usage times. This could then make the council 
vulnerable to offences such as credit-card or QR scams, as well as 
targeted vandalism of the machines to access cash payments. 

Reference:  IC-393971-B4K5 
 
 5 
28. He also considers that as the request identifies a specific named location 
and a very limited time period, the risk arising from disclosure is real. 
He is therefore satisfied that the prejudice being claimed is “real, actual 
or of substance”, and that there is a causal link between disclosure and 
the prejudice claimed. 
29. In addition, the exemption does not require the council to demonstrate 
that it is holding the information for the purpose of preventing and 
detecting crime, it only has to demonstrate that disclosing the 
information could harm efforts to prevent or detect it. 
30. Having considered the circumstances of this case, the Commissioner has 
therefore decided that section 31(1)(a) of FOIA is engaged. He has gone 
on to consider the public interest test. 
Public interest test 
The public interest in disclosing the information 
31. The council recognised that it has a duty to be transparent, and that it is 
“open and honest” in proactive publication of this type of information. 
32.  The complainant argued that the basis of their request was to discover 
the “total funds taken from the General Public without authority.” 
The public interest in maintaining the exemption 
33. The council explained that it already publishes this information Civil 
parking enforcement annual statistics and annual parking report on its 
website, and it provided the link to this in its initial response. However, 
it acknowledged that the published information is not at the granular 
level of detail requested. 
34. The council argued that it has a “duty to protect the public from 
potential fraud and loss of public money.” It stated that it is in the public 
interest to safeguard its revenues streams, such as those generated by 
car parking machines, from either an increased risk of vandalism and 
theft, including the theft of cash, as well as fraudulent methods 
involving credit card misuse or QR code scams. 
35. Any criminal activity, the council argued, would result in public expense 
to deal with the crime and loss of income. 
The balance of the public interest 
36. The Commissioner accepts that there is always a public interest in public 
authorities being open and transparent. 

Reference:  IC-393971-B4K5 
 
 6 
37. The Commissioner notes that this request stems from a complaint, 
submitted to the council, regarding alleged “misleading signs and a 
demand for payment.”  
38. However, the Commissioner considers that the requested information is 
of limited public interest. Its relevance is confined largely to the 
complainant and any other individual who may have parked in that 
carpark during the specific period of time. 
39. The Commissioner notes the complainant’s comment that “observations 
revealed many 100s” of payments being made. However, he considers 
that if an individual believes they have incurred an improper charge or 
fine, there are established and accessible routes for challenging such 
matters directly with the council, as the requester has already done. 
40. The Commissioner does not consider that the disclosure of the 
requested information would resolve or help with any person’s concerns 
regarding an unnecessary payment/and or potential fine. 
41. Instead, he considers that disclosure could make the council more 
vulnerable to acts of criminal activity through revealing financial details 
that could be used to facilitate fraud or other unlawful conduct. 
42. For these reasons, the Commissioner is satisfied that in all the 
circumstances of the case, the balance of the public interest is weighted 
in favour of maintaining the exemption.  Therefore, the council was 
entitled to rely on sections 31(1)(a) of FOIA to withhold the requested 
information. 

Reference:  IC-393971-B4K5 
 
 7 
Right of appeal  
 
43. Either party has the right to appeal against this decision notice to the 
First-tier Tribunal (Information Rights). Information about the appeals 
process may be obtained from:  
First-tier Tribunal (Information Rights) 
General Regulatory Chamber  
PO Box 11230 
Leicester  
LE1 8FQ 
 
Tel: 0203 936 8963 
Fax: 0870 739 5836 
Email: grc@justice.gov.uk  
Website: www.justice.gov.uk/tribunals/general-regulatory-
chamber  
 
44. If you wish to appeal against a decision notice, you can obtain 
information on how to appeal along with the relevant forms from the 
Information Tribunal website. 
45. Any Notice of Appeal should be served on the Tribunal within 28 
(calendar) days of the date on which this decision notice is sent. 
 
 
 
Victoria McGillicuddy 
Senior Case Officer 
Information Commissioner’s Office  
Wycliffe House  
Water Lane  
Wilmslow  
Cheshire  
SK9 5AF
description:
The complainant has requested information about the total amount collected by Bridlington Park and Ride parking machines during 5-12 April 2025. East Riding of Yorkshire (the council) refused to disclose the requested information citing section 31(1)(a) of FOIA (law enforcement) as its basis for doing so.
The Commissioner’s decision is that the council has correctly applied section 31(1)(a) of FOIA to withhold the requested information and that the public interest favours maintaining the exemption.
The Commissioner does not require further steps as a result of this decision notice.
